title: What Is the Digital-Money Debate Really About?
gist: Reichlin reframes the stablecoin/CBDC debate as a fight over the architecture of money, not technology, and proposes 'narrow banks' that issue money but not credit, hold reserves at the central bank, and pay capped interest to coin holders—with Europe's monetary sovereignty at stake.

insights.claim: The debate over stablecoins and central bank digital currencies is fundamentally about the architecture of money, not about technology.
insights.speaker: LUCREZIA REICHLIN
insights.so_what: Investors should read digital-money policy as a contest over who controls money creation, not as a fintech story—implications for banks, payment rails, and monetary sovereignty.
insights.quote: The debate over stablecoins and central bank digital currencies (CBDCs) may seem to be about technology, but it is actually about the architecture of money.

insights.claim: The current digital-money debate is a reopening of an old monetary-design question that most economists believed had been settled long ago, not a genuinely new debate.
insights.speaker: LUCREZIA REICHLIN
insights.so_what: Signals that historical narrow-banking and full-reserve debates are the relevant analytical frame for pricing stablecoin regulation outcomes.
insights.quote: the reopening of an issue that most economists thought had been settled long ago

insights.claim: Reichlin argues the right design is to let 'narrow banks'—entities that issue money but not credit—keep their reserves at the central bank and pay capped interest to coin holders.
insights.speaker: LUCREZIA REICHLIN
insights.so_what: A narrow-bank model separates payments from credit risk; watch whether EU/US regulation moves toward central-bank reserve backing and capped remuneration for stablecoins.
insights.quote: The key is to allow “narrow banks,” which issue money but not credit, to keep their reserves in central banks and provide capped interest payments to coin holders.
insights.locator: Both the US and Europe are moving

insights.claim: Both the US and Europe are moving to create digital money, but Reichlin contends neither has the right design approach.
insights.speaker: LUCREZIA REICHLIN
insights.so_what: A critique of both jurisdictions' current plans implies regulatory redesign risk; positioning in stablecoin issuers should account for architecture changes.
insights.locator: Both the US and Europe are moving

insights.claim: How the digital-money debate resolves will determine whose money Europeans ultimately end up using, making it a question of European monetary sovereignty.
insights.speaker: LUCREZIA REICHLIN
insights.so_what: If Europe fails to build a viable euro digital-money architecture, dollar-backed stablecoins could dominate European payments—a strategic and FX risk.
insights.quote: not least for determining whose money Europeans end up using
insights.locator: How this renewed debate plays out
